sentences.text: He was-- he was in uniform.
And-- but he wasn't in the high one.
He was just a policeman.
And I didn't see much of him.
He was doing his duty when they ordered to do this, or that, or that.
I remember-- I know that he helped some people because I met.
After the war, I met some woman in Israel.
And she said that she's alive thanks to my brother.
And it was a one-- Tyler, who complained that he slap his face.
But I knew that he wouldn't do this.
The German would kill him.
Because if they stopped the work, they were killed.
I know this.
But-- there have to be policemen.
The German demanded.
They don't-- they didn't do anything by themselves.
They didn't want to do anything by themselves.
My sister was in different barrack.
And I see her but not that much.
Not much.
See her when she walked to-- she was working at different factory.
As well, German-- I think in new uniform they send.
In Madritch, that was a better factory in Plaszow.
Madritch.
She worked at Madritch.
And she wasn't in the same barrack as I was, which was a pity because I miss her a lot.
I was very lonely in Plaszow with strangers.
Strangers around me.
And-- it was hungry.
I was hungry.
And I was tired.
Very sleepy.
That apparently they were tormenting us with calling us to appell and counting and counting.
And we stayed for hours and hours.
A row of five, usually.
And they count us and then we go back to our quarter.
And then was other selection.
There were other-- they tried to take people away again.
I don't know.
Sick people or older people.
And we-- yes, we were working.
And that the factory that [INAUDIBLE] shop was all the time when I was working.
Because we were sleepy.
We were tired.
And they called some of the girls to give blood.
Some of them.
I wasn't chosen.
But they did give some blood for-- Funny, did they give blood for German?
They said, it shouldn't be mixed blood.
Why did they do this?
I don't know.
They needed blood for-- for the solider, for the wounded maybe.
And they called people to give blood.
Do you know people who gave blood?
I remember just now.
Who took the-- if I know, I knew many.
From all around me, they were choosing this, this, this, and this.
They-- because I remember this now.
I don't know.
Maybe a year, or more than a year.
I really lost it.
I don't know the times, you know.
It's-- my sister know much better.
My sister, unfortunately, passed away two years ago, and I miss her terribly.
But she did remember better than I did.
I was-- I was much more scared.
I was more frightened that-- everybody was stronger somehow than I was.
My sister-in-law and the other one, they were stronger than I was.
I was-- I was always frightened somehow.
Fear.
I have fear in me all the time.
Well, then we supposed to go.
We know that we are going to Czechoslovakia to the camp called Brunnlitz.
We heard this.
There was talking.
It was whisper, and there was talking.
A list, a list, a list, is making.
A list.
And my sister-in-law knew that they are-- she is on the list with her husband.
That was my sister-in-law, Manci-- Henry's And she got that.
I didn't tell you this.
They got a little boy, Olek.
Olek was-- I don't know.
Four, five, or six.
And there when was-- choosing-- there were segregation, there were this-- they took.
They were taking children.
He hided with the other boys.
They hide in the latrine.
And a few times-- and he was still in Plaszow, Olek.
And he was on the list.
He was supposed to go to Brunnlitz.
Because what Schindler added to his people from the factory-- he had a factory outside ghetto.
Emalia, they call it.
Emalia because of Emily-- I don't know.
I suppose it depends on what they-- they said that they were making for the army these.
But he added to that.
What I heard after, he edit some people from children.
He said he need some more people that was with bigger factory.
And we'd been on the list.
And family as well.
There's been family, Rosner-- that was my husband's sister and niece, Niusia.
Niusia was younger, much younger.
She was maybe girl of 12 at that time.
And her sister, and his brother.
It was family.
And of course, people who were metalworker or whatever.
My brother-in-law who is alive here, he was in Brunnlitz.
I didn't know he would be my brother-in-law, because they met after the war.
But he was there.
And my brother was there.
And we were put on the wagon close.
Cattle train.
And it was close.
And it was very, very squeezed tight.
There were so many of us in that wagon.
That was after-- after Plaszow.
After Plaszow.
Well, it was in springtime.
I know it was spring.
I think.
No, no, no.
It wasn't spring.
Spring time wasn't right.
It was cold.
Rather cold.
It must be winter.
And it was the end of '43, I think.
Beginning of '44. '44 maybe.
It was winter.
And we were in this wagon what was close, and we were going in the wagon.
The train was moving, and then it stops, and then it's moving, and then it stops.
And it stopped for hours, and we didn't have anything to eat of that.
We were so squeezed we couldn't even lie down.
We could just stand or leading one on each other.
And, again, there wasn't toilet.
And it was in corner some bucket or whatever.
And it was stinks.
And it was dirty.
And-- and I was already sick.
I-- and-- I don't know if that was-- but I didn't turn it.
Did I tell you what from Plaszow?
Where did we go from Plaszow?
From Plaszow we went to-- we were supposed to go to Brunnlitz but we didn't.
We went to Auschwitz.
We landed in Auschwitz.
Why did you go to Auschwitz?
I don't know why.
That was a mistake.
They said, after the war that-- after, I found out that it was a mistake.
That it was-- that-- no, all 300 of us was in the group.
They were call it for Schindler.
And we didn't know where we going.
And when they open-- open eventually the door, we were so hungry and so.
It was Auschwitz.
It was winter.
And it was Birkenau, part of Auschwitz.
And it was a panic because the gas.
Warmer under the Nazi-- the chimney there.
They are burning.
That this smoke.
And I said, no, it's possible.
That the imagination-- that's not.
And-- and there was such a confusion.
It was awful.
They start to scream, [GERMAN SPEECH].
And we have to go out of that wagon, and we went in Birkenau.
Then it was very cold.
And they allocate us to some barracks.
And then they took us to wash house or something like this to be-- to be-- disinfected or deloused.
We have a lot.
And-- because we have lice already there.
And it was smell.
That smell comes from the chimney.
And we've seen this.
We seen even fire goes out of chimney.
And we spent that three weeks.
And if you ask me-- I really don't know-- it was three weeks of hell.
It was no food, no drink, and they scream, and they push us here and there.
And we didn't know what to do.
They didn't know what to do.
They call us.
There was the other Jewish people from other camp.
It must have been-- probably they were Hungarian, or Slovakian, something like this.
And they were already long time in Auschwitz.
And they call us, you Plaszow slut, or whatever, or witches.
They really were very terrible to us.
And again, the standing, and waiting, and counting us, and counting again, and we go back.
And-- and we were hungry.
I remember that was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] truck, I think, with vegetables or something passing by.
And the leaves were falling down.
And a few of the girl want to pick it.
And there were some of them-- not from our group, from the other group were shot-- because we shouldn't go out of our column.
And I was thirsty.
I was thirsty.
And I wanted to drink.
And in latrine, they said the water is contaminated-- how you call it, yeah?
You couldn't drink it because it's poison and it's typhoid.
But I was so thirsty that I drink it.
I even leaned and I drink from the gutter the water.
And I didn't know if I have-- I most probably I have typhoid already.
I was sick, because I didn't know much.
It was the last few days in Birkenau.
I was fevering.
And then what I heard that it was-- we didn't get a tattoo.
Some group went to have tattoo with numbers and our group didn't.
And everybody said because we are going to be burning.
You are going to oven.
We are going to be gassed.
We are going to die.
But one day, it was called.
We were called by names.
And I had that German come.
And they called in name.
Manci Rosner, Regina Horowitz, Niusia Horowitz, Helena Rosner.
And so there were 300 names were called.
And we were put aside.
And we're supposed to go to the wagon.
It was high and I couldn't reach that.
I know somebody lift me most probably.
And I don't remember much in that wagon.
I know that we was squeezed again, and we were hungry.
But we arrived to Brunnlitz.
He was already in Brunnlitz.
But he was-- between Plaszow, he went to Gross-Rosen.
Again, not direct to Brunnlitz.
He was in Gross-Rosen.
I find this out after this.
I didn't know.
I didn't know where he is.
But when we've been at-- when we've been at Brunnlitz, I knew there are some man somewhere.
And they greet us.
I don't know.
I start to talk to my husband through the hole in the door or-- and I fainted.
And that's it.
My sister wasn't on the list.
No, she wasn't.
My brother was but not my sister.
It was some-- again, I find it out after that's scratching some people, because everybody wants to be on the list.
That some of them know, you know.
Some Jewish people know that this bribery.
That you have to have some money, or diamond, or whatever to be on the list.
But I didn't-- at that time, I didn't know about all these thing.
I know that my sister wasn't there.
She was in the beginning, and then she was thrown out with many others.
To Auschwitz.
And when I was in Birkenau, for only one moment I seen her from distance in another column.
For a moment, I seen her in Auschwitz.
And then I lost completely.
I didn't know in Brunnlitz if she's alive.
If I got sister or I didn't.
In Birkenau, nobody.
I was not that sick.
I was just sick.
But girls helped me, most probably.
Women around me.
But I was really collapsing in Brunnlitz.
That I lost-- that I was in coma, unconscious for a few days, and I have typhoid.
And that in Brunnlitz already there were some girl and looked after me.
There was one of them with our group who was a nurse.
She lives here in [INAUDIBLE].
I don't meet her very often, [NON-ENGLISH], I think she said, after her husband.
But they given me some drink.
And in Brunnlitz, they know that was already no shooting.
We didn't hear any-- shooting.
We didn't have-- it wasn't burning, it wasn't beating.
No, no one was beaten up there.
It was strict, but we were told to go there.
And there in the beginning, we live in-- we sleep on the floor on this straw.
And then was bank.
And I was with my sister-in-law on one bank.
Next to me, was the other lady who is still in Melbourne.
And she was in the next to me.
And we work in the factory.
Ammunition, it was ammunition factory.
But that was after a few days.
Maybe a week I was very, very sick.
I was so sick.
I don't-- I remember that I was feverish.
I remember that my sister-in-laws talked to each other, nobody can help her.
I think only God can help her.
And-- ah, yes.
My husband somehow find.
He knew that I am sick, and he got some apples, and they cooked some stewed apples for me.
Only there were pipe, a hot pipe.
On this they cooked it.
And they fed me with that.
But again, lice.
I got a lot of lice in my hair.
I wasn't shaven.
And my pubic hair, and under my arms.
And it was somebody got a comb, a two-sided comb, very fine.
But we have to borrow this.
It was not easy to get it, but I have that.
The lice were alive, and big, and it was awful.
That was hurting.
That was beating.
And I know that somehow after, we were brought some stuff which was disinfected or whatever in Brunnlitz for those few who are sick.
And that passed it, the Typhoid.
It wasn't anymore.
That was in the beginning.
And after this, I start to work in the factory.
And the ammunition, it was a metal.
I don't know.
It was a name of the factory in Brunnlitz.
I don't remember the name.
Schindler's place.
And metal.
And there were-- for bullets.
That covered from bullets, [NON-ENGLISH], as we called it.
I was working on that.
It was very hot metal.
And it was electric machine.
And you have to put it, fill it in, and take it out like a cover.
And I was doing this.
My hand was very cracked and cut with this metal.
But it was.
And we knew that there wasn't an danger of life danger.
And in the beginning, we have bread.
It was cut in eight pieces.
Eight of a the lot of bread for each of one.
And soup.
And coffee in the morning.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- when-- it-- after this Birkenau, and after-- it was good comparatively.
Later on in Brunnlitz, it was less and less food.
Much less food.
I know that I didn't know what to eat.
Whole portion at once or-- I start to keep it for later on, but I never have enough time, enough will power.
I ate it.
And my husband started to play again.
He got his accordion in Brunnlitz.
And he play for Schindler, for his party.
I didn't know about that, but I knew that he'd played.
And he gets sometimes sausage and sometimes piece of bread.
And he brought to me these things.
And it was funny, that Regina-- that was my husband's sister-- he didn't [INAUDIBLE].
She didn't get used to that I'm his wife.
That I'm the one who's supposed to get this, and then I share it between my other sister-in-law, Niusia, [NON-ENGLISH], all of them.
I have to share it, that piece of sausage between us.
I know it was Pesach once.
And some of the Jews, I heard of this.
I knew they got some even matzoh that Schindler give-- find somewhere for them.
He was-- Schindler was coming on-- I never knew-- Emilie Schindler was there as well.
Schindler's wife-- but we haven't seen her much.
She was behind.
She was in the kitchen.
She was helping.
And he was a smoker.
And he smoked cigarettes.
And he always started and throw it that Jewish people can pick up and smoke.
I smoked that time, too.
I started to smoke.
I started to smoke.
And I know that my husband-- he likes my husband, because he give him sometimes food that I could share with the others.
Yeah.
How could you celebrate?
We didn't.
We knew it is, but we didn't celebrate specially.
It was rather sad, because we still didn't know what will happen to us.
We know that at that moment, in Brunnlitz it was better than the other.
But what will be on the end?
We didn't think about it, you see.
We didn't think.
What-- I don't know what the others.
But myself, I was just hungry.
And I want to eat.
And I was sleepy.
And I want to see my husband.
And that all what I was thinking of.
I didn't have any plans.
Nearly every day but not directly.
It was a balcony again.
It was divide-- divided by a wooden fence or whatever.
And it was this-- between that-- how you call it?
That little space.
And then we can talk.
And we push each other that whoever can get closer and talk.
That's how we talked.
How are we communicated.
Well, I sometimes see him on the factory.
In the factory, it was a big room.
A big factory.
I seen him from a distance but not much.
Yes.
Yes, we see Schindler quite often.
He was very handsome.
And he even speak a few of the words in Polish, because he was Czechoslovakian.
And he was smiling often.
He was such an authority.
It was such a-- like a sun will come to the factory when Schindler walked.
And there was special girl looks up to him.
He was handsome.
He was good looking.
And-- and he was the one who we knew that we can trust him, because he never have a gun with him.
He never wore a uniform.
He was in civil clothes.
He was wearing hat.
Tyrolean hat sometimes.
Oh, yes.
Once, I remember that comes to Brunnlitz near the end of-- near the end of the war, a wagon with people from others from somewhere.
And it was frozen.
And Mrs.
Schindler ordered that to stop it.
And the man went and tried to open, the wagon was frozen.
And there were some people who are looking not like a people, like animal.
Like frozen dead bodies.
And I know I had that ammunition.
I cooked some porridge or whatever.
And whoever was alive, you know to live, and went through the war that finished.
But those many dead bodies.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was Brunnlitz.
How many was there?
Women-- were 300 women was.
And I think 700 men-- no more.
There were 1,000.
More than 700.
Altogether, there was 1,200, and 1,300, or something like that.
But all whoever in Brunnlitz were alive.
No one was dead.
Some were sick.
My husband got sick on the very end.
On the last day of the war, he got temperature.
And he didn't go down when we all went down to see.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- anyhow, that even the end of the war is like-- for me.
I don't exactly remember every details.
Well, it was on top of the factory, I believe.
On top of the factory.
And the pipe goes through that-- between our [NON-ENGLISH] we called it.
I don't know how you call it.
We slept.
It was divided.
And it was quite warm.
It was good, because it was winter time, and we lived close to the warm pipe.
That was not better.
It was too warm.
And my sister-in-law, Manci, she was much older than I was.
And she was good to me.
She-- she helped me morally.
She was cheering me up.
She talks to me.
And-- and she-- from-- from Birkenau, we come with rags.
I remember that in Birkenau, we have to give all of our whatever we have-- that uniform-- whatever we have.
And we get after that being in the shower in Birkenau, which we don't know it was shower.
Then we are getting some rags or whatever.
Clothes what's left on the other.
And I had a nighty or something.
It was cold.
And Manci fitted for me somehow.
She helps me to do it as a frock in Brunnlitz.
Yes, I was very sick.
In Brunnlitz, that was that what I get from Birkenau.
It was changed to frock.
Manci was very clever.
And it-- Brunnlitz was a-- actually, that Brunnlitz camp was on a-- before it was some knitting factory.
And they go and they find that men find some wool.
We got wool, very thick wool.
And the men make knitting needles for us.
And I start to knit, and Manci taught me.
I even have a five needle to make a socks.
And I don't know how I would be able to do it now, especially with my shaking hand.
But I was knitting, and we knitted jumpers.
All we had to first-- you know, undo it because it was all the worn and things.
And then start to knit again.
And so that's-- yeah.
Oh, when we have to shower again was we have to have in turn showers, you know.
In turn because there wasn't enough.
And under the shower, we washed whatever we have to wear.
And we can dry on that pipe.
That was hot pipe.
And you could dry that.
Even yes, sometimes a piece of bread we can put it on the pipe and make it hot.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Until the end of the war.
That was in May 8 or 9-- 8 or 5-- 5th of May.
I don't know.
That was I don't know how long.
It was maybe half a year, six months, or something like this.
You mean finishing?
Well, I don't know what the freedom is.
We knew that the war finish.
Everybody around us.
What The German disappear.
The Germans start to disappear, whoever was that.
And we knew that Schindler was the day before he knew already.
And he was-- they make for him that stripes prisoner uniform or they call it.
And they put in him.
And for Mrs.-- for Emilie.
And this-- oh, I didn't tell you about the ring what he got, Schindler.
Yes.
It was my brother-in-law-- who is now my brother-in-law, but I didn't know that he will be.
He was and he make the ring for Schindler from the teeth, I believe.
Some boys, some men.
Men-- they were men, but they were young people.
They give the crown, they call it, from the teeth, from the tooth.
A gold tooth.
And then he make a ring for Schindler.
And when it was Schindler's birthday, they bake a cake from-- I don't know-- from flour from potato flour or-- I don't know what flour.
And my niece present him.
I mean, it was Niusia Horowitz come with the cake from the-- Herr Schindler.
And he kiss her.
He kiss her, but was-- he wasn't allowed.
He was-- some other German was watching.
And he went to jail, I think, for a day or two-- Schindler.
He wasn't allowed to kiss.
But she did it.
Niusia did, yes.
She's now in Poland.
She lives in Krakow.
We visit Krakow five months-- five years ago.
She is beautician, and she's a lovely woman.
She married a Christian.
She married a Catholic boy.
So he said that they were-- they made a striped uniform for Schindler.
Yes.
How many what?
What I can tell you, I-- again, it's hazy.
Very hazy.
I knew that was a big car, and the Jewish people put in.
I know that there was one woman, Helen, what's her name.
Helen.
She was a maid for, you know.
She was in the building, but she used to be maid for Goeth.
And he get her to Brunnlitz.
She was saved this way.
And I know they disappeared.
They left in a car, Schindler with his wife.
And I think some Jewish, two Jewish men, as well.
I don't know their name.
I heard his name, but I forgotten it.
One was Pollack.
He was cleaning his shoes.
He had shoes, and was in list as well.
And then where left-- we'd been alone.
No one was there for a day, I think.
There was no German.
It was no one.
We were-- we didn't looking.
No one from-- [INAUDIBLE] no one from Brunnlitz, from the country come closer to the camp side where we were.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- ah, yes.
And before he left, this Schindler, he make a speech.
He call us all.
That I do remember.
And my husband was sick.
I think he didn't go to that, because he'd got temperature.
